content.text:
This is **Redmine**, a Ruby on Rails project-management application. Here's the orientation map for the session.

## What it is
Redmine — a mature Rails app (server-rendered, MVC). Version in `lib/redmine/version.rb`.

## Layout

**Domain models** — `app/models/` (84 files). The ones central to the upcoming visibility/permissions change:
- `app/models/issue.rb` — the core entity (issue visibility scopes live here)
- `app/models/project.rb` — projects own the access boundary
- `app/models/user.rb`, `app/models/principal.rb`, `app/models/group*.rb` — actors
- `app/models/role.rb`, `app/models/member.rb`, `app/models/member_role.rb` — the membership↔permission join
- `app/models/concerns/` — shared model mixins

**Controllers** — `app/controllers/` (57 files):
- `app/controllers/application_controller.rb` — base class; auth filters, `authorize`, `find_project`, etc.
- `app/controllers/issues_controller.rb`, `context_menus_controller.rb`, `journals_controller.rb` — issue-facing surfaces
- Supporting: `helpers/`, `views/`, `jobs/`, `validators/`

**Shared library code** — `lib/redmine/` is the heart of cross-cutting infrastructure. For permissions specifically:
- `lib/redmine/access_control.rb` — **the permission registry** (defines all permissions, modules, public actions)
- `lib/redmine/access_keys.rb` — API/RSS key access
- `lib/redmine/safe_attributes.rb` — mass-assignment guarding (ties into who-can-edit-what)
- `lib/redmine/sudo_mode.rb`, `lib/redmine/menu_manager.rb`, `lib/redmine/search.rb`
- `lib/redmine.rb` — bootstraps the registry: registers permissions, menus, and activity providers at load time

**Extension / plugin surface**:
- `lib/redmine/plugin.rb` + `lib/redmine/plugin_loader.rb` — plugin definition & loading
- `lib/redmine/hook.rb` + `lib/redmine/hook/` — view & controller hook points
- `plugins/` — drop-in plugin directory (currently just a README)
- `lib/redmine/field_format.rb`, `lib/redmine/wiki_formatting.rb`, `lib/redmine/twofa.rb`, `lib/redmine/scm/` — pluggable subsystem registries
- `themes/` — theming surface

## Key takeaway for the visibility/permissions change
The permission system is **two-layered**: (1) a static registry in `lib/redmine/access_control.rb` (declared via `lib/redmine.rb`), and (2) per-record visibility enforced in the models — chiefly `Issue#visible?` / `Issue.visible` scope in `app/models/issue.rb`, gated by `Project` + `Role`/`Member`. Controllers enforce via `authorize` in `application_controller.rb`. Plugins extend all of this through `Redmine::Plugin.register` and hooks.

Next I'll drill into the permission registry (`access_control.rb` + `redmine.rb`) and how `Role` consumes it.

# Returns a SQL conditions string used to find all issues visible by the specified user
  def self.visible_condition(user, options={})
    Project.allowed_to_condition(user, :view_issues, options) do |role, user|
      sql =
        if user.id && user.logged?
          case role.issues_visibility
          when 'all'
            '1=1'
          when 'default'
            user_ids = [user.id] + user.groups.pluck(:id).compact
            "(#{table_name}.is_private = #{connection.quoted_false} " \
              "OR #{table_name}.author_id = #{user.id} " \
              "OR #{table_name}.assigned_to_id IN (#{user_ids.join(',')}))"
          when 'own'
            user_ids = [user.id] + user.groups.pluck(:id).compact
            "(#{table_name}.author_id = #{user.id} OR " \
              "#{table_name}.assigned_to_id IN (#{user_ids.join(',')}))"
          else
            '1=0'
          end
        else
          "(#{table_name}.is_private = #{connection.quoted_false})"
        end
      unless role.permissions_all_trackers?(:view_issues)
        tracker_ids = role.permissions_tracker_ids(:view_issues)
        if tracker_ids.any?
          sql = "(#{sql} AND #{table_name}.tracker_id IN (#{tracker_ids.join(',')}))"
        else
          sql = '1=0'
        end
      end
      sql
    end
  end

  # Returns true if usr or current user is allowed to view the issue
  def visible?(usr=nil)
    (usr || User.current).allowed_to?(:view_issues, self.project) do |role, user|
      visible =
        if user.logged?
          case role.issues_visibility
          when 'all'
            true
          when 'default'
            !self.is_private? || (self.author == user || user.is_or_belongs_to?(assigned_to))
          when 'own'
            self.author == user || user.is_or_belongs_to?(assigned_to)
          else
            false
          end
        else
          !self.is_private?
        end
      unless role.permissions_all_trackers?(:view_issues)
        visible &&= role.permissions_tracker_ids?(:view_issues, tracker_id)
      end
      visible
    end
  end

  # Returns true if user or current user is allowed to log time on the issue
  def time_loggable?(user=User.current)
    user.allowed_to?(:log_time, project) && (Setting.timelog_accept_closed_issues? || !closed?)
  end

  # Returns true if user or current user is allowed to edit or add notes to the issue
  def editable?(user=User.current)
    attributes_editable?(user) || notes_addable?(user)
  end

  # Returns true if user or current user is allowed to edit the issue
  def attributes_editable?(user=User.current)
    user_tracker_permission?(user, :edit_issues) || (
      user_tracker_permission?(user, :edit_own_issues) && author == user
    )
  end

  def attachments_addable?(user=User.current)
    attributes_editable?(user) || notes_addable?(user)
  end

  # Overrides Redmine::Acts::Attachable::InstanceMethods#attachments_editable?
  def attachments_editable?(user=User.current)
    visible?(user) && attributes_editable?(user)
  end

  # Returns true if user or current user is allowed to add notes to the issue
  def notes_addable?(user=User.current)
    user_tracker_permission?(user, :add_issue_notes)
  end

  # Returns true if user or current user is allowed to delete the issue
  def deletable?(user=User.current)
    user_tracker_permission?(user, :delete_issues)
  end

Here is the contract map for `app/models/issue.rb` (class declared at line 19, `class Issue < ApplicationRecord`).

## Shared "plugin-style" behaviors mixed in

These are the engines that the visibility/permissions change will ripple through. The `acts_as_*` macros live in `lib/plugins/*` (bundled engines) and `lib/redmine/acts/*`:

| Mixin | Declared at | Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| `include Redmine::SafeAttributes` | issue.rb:21 | `lib/redmine/safe_attributes.rb` — **mass-assignment gate; permission-keyed** (see `safe_attributes` blocks issue.rb:498–537) |
| `include Redmine::Utils::DateCalculation` | issue.rb:22 | `lib/redmine/utils.rb` |
| `include Redmine::I18n` | issue.rb:23 | `lib/redmine/i18n.rb` |
| `include Redmine::NestedSet::IssueNestedSet` | issue.rb:29 | `lib/redmine/nested_set/issue_nested_set.rb` — parent/child tree |
| `include Redmine::Reaction::Reactable` | issue.rb:30 | `lib/redmine/reaction.rb` |
| `acts_as_attachable` | issue.rb:48 | `lib/plugins/acts_as_attachable/lib/acts_as_attachable.rb` — **note Issue overrides `attachments_editable?` (issue.rb:218) to AND with `visible?`** |
| `acts_as_customizable` | issue.rb:49 | `lib/plugins/acts_as_customizable/lib/acts_as_customizable.rb` — custom field values |
| `acts_as_watchable` | issue.rb:50 | `lib/plugins/acts_as_watchable/lib/acts_as_watchable.rb` — watchers feed notifications & `visible_watcher_users` |
| `acts_as_searchable` | issue.rb:51 | `lib/plugins/acts_as_searchable/lib/acts_as_searchable.rb` — **search uses `visible_condition` for permission scoping** |
| `acts_as_event` | issue.rb:55 | `lib/plugins/acts_as_event/lib/acts_as_event.rb` |
| `acts_as_activity_provider` | issue.rb:59 | `lib/plugins/acts_as_activity_provider/lib/acts_as_activity_provider.rb` — **activity feed re-applies a visibility scope** |
| `acts_as_mentionable` | issue.rb:62 | `lib/redmine/acts/mentionable.rb` |
| `acts_as_webhookable` + `include Issue::Webhookable` | issue.rb:63–64 | `lib/redmine/acts/webhookable.rb` + `app/models/issue/webhookable.rb` |

## Associations (the relational fan-out)
- `belongs_to`: `project` (32), `tracker` (33), `status`→IssueStatus (34), `author`→User (35), `assigned_to`→**Principal** (36, can be a Group), `fixed_version`→Version (37), `priority` (38), `category` (39)
- `has_many`: `journals` (polymorphic journalized, 41), `time_entries` (42), `relations_from`/`relations_to`→IssueRelation (45–46)
- `has_and_belongs_to_many :changesets` (43)
- Plus association-driven: custom values, attachments, watchers (via the `acts_as_*` above)

## Cross-cutting concerns that shape the permission contract (the must-read core)
This is what a teammate changing visibility/permissions must internalize:

1. **`Issue.visible_condition(user, options)` — issue.rb:137–169.** The SQL form. Delegates to `Project.allowed_to_condition(user, :view_issues, ...)` and branches on `role.issues_visibility` (`'all'` / `'default'` / `'own'`), keyed on `is_private`, `author_id`, `assigned_to_id` (incl. user's groups), then further narrowed by per-tracker `view_issues` permission (issue.rb:159–166).
2. **`Issue#visible?(usr)` — issue.rb:172–194.** The in-Ruby mirror of #1 (must stay in sync — same `issues_visibility` branches, same tracker check). Divergence here is the classic bug.
3. **`scope :visible` — issue.rb:84–88** wraps `visible_condition` for query use.
4. **Edit/action gates, all routed through `user_tracker_permission?`:** `editable?` (202), `attributes_editable?` (207, uses `edit_issues`/`edit_own_issues`), `notes_addable?` (223, `add_issue_notes`), `deletable?` (228, `delete_issues`), `attachments_editable?` (218, ANDs `visible?`), `time_loggable?` (197).
5. **`validate_permissions` — validation hook (issue.rb:82)** enforces permission at save.
6. **`safe_attributes` blocks (issue.rb:498–537)** — each block is permission-gated, deciding which fields a role may mass-assign.

## Key takeaways for the change
- Visibility is defined **twice** — SQL (`visible_condition`) and Ruby (`visible?`) — and both branch on **`Role#issues_visibility`** plus **per-tracker permissions** (`role.permissions_all_trackers?` / `permissions_tracker_ids`). Both must change together.
- The real authority lives one level down in `Project.allowed_to_condition` and `User#allowed_to?` / `Role` — that's the next subsystem to map.
- `assigned_to` is a **Principal** (Group or User), so "own/assigned" checks use `user.is_or_belongs_to?` and group-id expansion — any visibility rewrite must preserve group semantics.
- Search and activity-feed surfaces independently consume `visible_condition`, so a change there is not just the issues controller.

Next (Task 3) I'll drop into `Role`, `Project.allowed_to_condition`, and `User#allowed_to?` to nail down the authority layer these all delegate to.

content.text:
This is the complete dependent map for `Issue`. I've split it into **two tiers**: things that touch the visibility/permission API (your hot path) and general structural dependents.

## TIER 1 — Visibility/permission API consumers (the regression-critical set)

These call `Issue.visible`, `Issue.visible_condition`, or mirror the same logic. **Every one must be re-checked when the visibility contract changes:**

**Direct `Issue.visible_condition(...)` (raw-SQL embedders — highest risk, they splice the SQL string):**
- `app/models/journal.rb:73` — `where(Issue.visible_condition(user, options))` → Journal visibility *is* issue visibility
- `app/models/issue_custom_field.rb:42` — ANDs `Issue.visible_condition(user)` into custom-field value lookups
- `app/models/issue_query.rb:62` — **string-rewrites** the SQL: `.gsub(/\bissues\b/, 'subtasks')` for subtask counts — fragile to any table-alias change
- `app/models/time_entry.rb:70` — `TimeEntry.visible_condition` embeds `Issue.visible_condition` (time entries inherit issue visibility)

**`Issue.visible` scope (controllers):**
- `issues_controller.rb:425, 597`; `previews_controller.rb:25`; `projects_controller.rb:186-191`; `repositories_controller.rb:265`; `search_controller.rb:41`; `users_controller.rb:101-106`; `versions_controller.rb:53`

**`Issue.visible` scope (models/helpers/lib):**
- `issue_relation.rb:96`; `issue_query.rb:376`; `app/helpers/application_helper.rb:1195`; `issues_helper.rb:584,592`; `my_helper.rb:80`; `lib/redmine/wiki_formatting/macros.rb:368`

**Upstream authority `Issue` delegates to (change these and Issue moves):**
- `app/models/project.rb:178` `Project.visible_condition` + `Project.allowed_to_condition` (called from `Issue.visible_condition` issue.rb:138)
- `app/models/role.rb` — `issues_visibility`, `permissions_all_trackers?`, `permissions_tracker_ids` (Task 2)
- `app/models/user.rb` / `principal.rb:150` — `allowed_to?`, `is_or_belongs_to?`

**Parallel `visible?`/`visible_condition` implementations to keep semantically aligned** (sibling records whose visibility is defined relative to issues): `time_entry.rb:87,100`, `journal.rb:165`, `issue_relation.rb:103`, plus the broader family at `attachment.rb:205`, `document.rb`, `version.rb:198`, `board.rb`, `message.rb`, `news.rb`, `query.rb:413`.

## TIER 2 — Structural dependents (associations, behavior, rendering)

**Controllers (16):** `application_controller.rb` (find/authorize plumbing), `issues_controller.rb`, `context_menus_controller.rb`, `journals_controller.rb`, `auto_completes_controller.rb`, `issue_relations_controller.rb`, `issue_categories_controller.rb`, `issue_statuses_controller.rb`, `reports_controller.rb`, `timelog_controller.rb`, `versions_controller.rb`, `projects_controller.rb`, `repositories_controller.rb`, `search_controller.rb`, `users_controller.rb`, `previews_controller.rb`

**Models (20):** `project.rb`, `user.rb`, `group.rb`, `journal.rb`, `time_entry.rb`, `time_entry_query.rb`, `time_entry_import.rb`, `query.rb`, `issue_query.rb`, `issue_relation.rb`, `issue_category.rb`, `issue_status.rb`, `issue_custom_field.rb`, `issue_import.rb`, `tracker.rb`, `version.rb`, `changeset.rb`, `mail_handler.rb`, `mailer.rb`, `concerns/issue/webhookable.rb`

**Jobs:** none reference `Issue` by classname directly, but reach it indirectly — `app/jobs/` mailer/webhook delivery jobs operate on issues via `Mailer` and `acts_as_webhookable`. (Worth a second look in Task 7; the grep was clean but `Mailer.deliver_issue_*` is enqueued.)

**Helpers (21):** notably `issues_helper.rb`, `context_menus_helper.rb`, `queries_helper.rb`, `application_helper.rb`, `journals_helper.rb`, `gantt_helper.rb`, `reports_helper.rb`, `search_helper.rb`, `watchers_helper.rb`, `timelog_helper.rb`, plus the rest.

**Library code (13):**
- `lib/plugins/acts_as_searchable/lib/acts_as_searchable.rb` — search applies the visibility scope generically
- `lib/redmine/helpers/gantt.rb`, `lib/redmine/helpers/time_report.rb` — reporting over issues
- `lib/redmine/export/pdf/issues_pdf_helper.rb` — PDF export
- `lib/redmine/nested_set/issue_nested_set.rb` — parent/child tree
- `lib/redmine/field_format.rb` — custom field rendering of issue links
- `lib/redmine/wiki_formatting/macros.rb` — `{{issue}}` macro
- `lib/redmine/reaction.rb`, `lib/redmine/preparation.rb`, `lib/redmine/plugin.rb`, `lib/redmine/default_data/loader.rb`
- Rake: `lib/tasks/email.rake`, `migrate_from_mantis.rake`, `migrate_from_trac.rake`

def self.visible_condition(user, options={})
    allowed_to_condition(user, :view_project, options)
  end

  # Returns a SQL conditions string used to find all projects for which +user+ has the given +permission+
  #
  # Valid options:
  # * :skip_pre_condition => true       don't check that the module is enabled (eg. when the condition is already set elsewhere in the query)
  # * :project => project               limit the condition to project
  # * :with_subprojects => true         limit the condition to project and its subprojects
  # * :member => true                   limit the condition to the user projects
  def self.allowed_to_condition(user, permission, options={})
    perm = Redmine::AccessControl.permission(permission)
    base_statement =
      if perm && perm.read?
        "#{Project.table_name}.status IN (#{Project::STATUS_ACTIVE}, #{Project::STATUS_CLOSED})"
      else
        "#{Project.table_name}.status = #{Project::STATUS_ACTIVE}"
      end
    if !options[:skip_pre_condition] && perm && perm.project_module
      # If the permission belongs to a project module, make sure the module is enabled
      base_statement +=
        " AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 AS one FROM #{EnabledModule.table_name} em" \
          " WHERE em.project_id = #{Project.table_name}.id" \
          " AND em.name='#{perm.project_module}')"
    end
    if project = options[:project]
      project_statement = project.project_condition(options[:with_subprojects])
      base_statement = "(#{project_statement}) AND (#{base_statement})"
    end

    if user.admin?
      base_statement
    else
      statement_by_role = {}
      unless options[:member]
        role = user.builtin_role
        if role.allowed_to?(permission)
          s = "#{Project.table_name}.is_public = #{connection.quoted_true}"
          if user.id
            group = role.anonymous? ? Group.anonymous : Group.non_member
            principal_ids = [user.id, group.id].compact
            s =
              "(#{s} AND #{Project.table_name}.id NOT IN " \
                "(SELECT project_id FROM #{Member.table_name} " \
                "WHERE user_id IN (#{principal_ids.join(',')})))"
          end
          statement_by_role[role] = s
        end
      end
      user.project_ids_by_role.each do |role, project_ids|
        if role.allowed_to?(permission) && project_ids.any?
          statement_by_role[role] = "#{Project.table_name}.id IN (#{project_ids.join(',')})"
        end
      end
      if statement_by_role.empty?
        "1=0"
      else
        if block_given?
          statement_by_role.each do |role, statement|
            if s = yield(role, user)
              statement_by_role[role] = "(#{statement} AND (#{s}))"
            end
          end
        end
        "((#{base_statement}) AND (#{statement_by_role.values.join(' OR ')}))"
      end
    end
  end

def allowed_to?(action, context, options={}, &block)
    if context && context.is_a?(Project)
      return false unless context.allows_to?(action)
      # Admin users are authorized for anything else
      return true if admin?

      roles = roles_for_project(context)
      return false unless roles

      roles.any? do |role|
        (context.is_public? || role.member?) &&
        role.allowed_to?(action, @oauth_scope) &&
        (block ? yield(role, self) : true)
      end
    elsif context && context.is_a?(Array)
      if context.empty?
        false
      else
        # Authorize if user is authorized on every element of the array
        context.map {|project| allowed_to?(action, project, options, &block)}.reduce(:&)
      end
    elsif context
      raise ArgumentError.new("#allowed_to? context argument must be a Project, an Array of projects or nil")
    elsif options[:global]
      # Admin users are always authorized
      return true if admin?

      # authorize if user has at least one role that has this permission
      roles = self.roles.to_a | [builtin_role]
      roles.any? do |role|
        role.allowed_to?(action, @oauth_scope) &&
        (block ? yield(role, self) : true)
      end
    else
      false
    end
  end

  # Is the user allowed to do the specified action on any project?
  # See allowed_to? for the actions and valid options.
  #
  # NB: this method is not used anywhere in the core codebase as of
  # 2.5.2, but it's used by many plugins so if we ever want to remove
  # it it has to be carefully deprecated for a version or two.

def has_permission?(perm)
    !permissions.nil? && permissions.include?(perm.to_sym)
  end

  def consider_workflow?
    has_permission?(:add_issues) || has_permission?(:edit_issues)
  end

  def <=>(role)
    # returns -1 for nil since r2726
    return -1 if role.nil?
    return nil unless role.is_a?(Role)

    if builtin == role.builtin
      position <=> role.position
    else
      builtin <=> role.builtin
    end
  end

  def to_s
    name
  end

  def name
    case builtin
    when 1 then l(:label_role_non_member, :default => read_attribute(:name))
    when 2 then l(:label_role_anonymous,  :default => read_attribute(:name))
    else
      read_attribute(:name)
    end
  end

  # Return true if the role is a builtin role
  def builtin?
    self.builtin != 0
  end

  # Return true if the role is the anonymous role
  def anonymous?
    builtin == 2
  end

  # Return true if the role is a project member role
  def member?
    !self.builtin?
  end

  # Return true if role is allowed to do the specified action
  # action can be:
  # * a parameter-like Hash (eg. :controller => 'projects', :action => 'edit')
  # * a permission Symbol (eg. :edit_project)
  # scope can be:
  # * an array of permissions which will be used as filter (logical AND)

  def allowed_to?(action, scope=nil)
    if action.is_a? Hash
      allowed_actions(scope).include? "#{action[:controller]}/#{action[:action]}"
    else
      allowed_permissions(scope).include? action
    end
  end

  # Return all the permissions that can be given to the role
  def setable_permissions
    setable_permissions = Redmine::AccessControl.permissions - Redmine::AccessControl.public_permissions
    setable_permissions -= Redmine::AccessControl.members_only_permissions if self.builtin == BUILTIN_NON_MEMBER
    setable_permissions -= Redmine::AccessControl.loggedin_only_permissions if self.builtin == BUILTIN_ANONYMOUS
    setable_permissions
  end

  def permissions_tracker_ids(*args)
    if args.any?
      Array(permissions_tracker_ids[args.first.to_s]).map(&:to_i)
    else
      super || {}
    end
  end

  def permissions_tracker_ids=(arg)
    h = arg.to_hash
    h.each_value {|v| v.reject!(&:blank?)}
    super(h)
  end

  # Returns true if tracker_id belongs to the list of
  # trackers for which permission is given
  def permissions_tracker_ids?(permission, tracker_id)
    return false unless has_permission?(permission)

    permissions_tracker_ids(permission).include?(tracker_id)
  end

  def permissions_all_trackers
    super || {}
  end

  def permissions_all_trackers=(arg)
    super(arg.to_hash)
  end

I now have the full authority chain. Here's the complete trace of "may user U see issue I."

## The decision flow — two parallel paths that must agree

Redmine answers the visibility question **two ways**, and the heart of your change is that both encode the *same* rule:

```
SQL path  (bulk / queries):   Issue.visible_condition → Project.allowed_to_condition → Role.allowed_to? + yielded issue-SQL
Ruby path (single record):    Issue#visible?         → User#allowed_to?            → Role.allowed_to? + yielded issue-bool
```

Both funnel through the **same two authorities**: `Project`/`User` (project-level gate) and `Role` (permission + `issues_visibility` + tracker), differing only in *form* (SQL string vs. boolean).

## 1. Where the visibility rule lives — `Role`
The rule's *parameters* are columns on `Role`:
- `issues_visibility` enum — values `'all'` / `'default'` (public) / `'own'`, defined `app/models/role.rb:40-44`, validated `role.rb:86-88`, serialized in safe-attrs list `role.rb:102`.
- `permissions_tracker_ids` (per-permission tracker allowlist) — `role.rb:220-240`; the predicate `permissions_tracker_ids?(permission, tracker_id)` at `role.rb:236-240` first requires `has_permission?` (`role.rb:149-151`).
- `permissions_all_trackers?(permission)` — `role.rb:251`.
- `Role#allowed_to?(action, scope)` — `role.rb:204-210` → `allowed_permissions(scope).include?(action)`; this is where the `:view_issues` permission is actually checked.

## 2. The database-level condition — `Issue.visible_condition`
`app/models/issue.rb:137-169` (mapped in Task 2). It calls **`Project.allowed_to_condition(user, :view_issues, options)` with a block** (issue.rb:138). The block returns the per-role issue-SQL:
- branches on `role.issues_visibility` → `'all'`⇒`1=1`; `'default'`⇒`is_private=false OR author_id=U OR assigned_to_id IN (U+groups)`; `'own'`⇒`author OR assigned`; else `1=0` (issue.rb:141-155)
- anonymous/not-logged-in ⇒ `is_private = false` (issue.rb:156-158)
- then ANDs the per-tracker `view_issues` allowlist (issue.rb:159-166).

**`Project.allowed_to_condition`** — `app/models/project.rb:189-246` — is the project-level gate the block plugs into:
- builds a `base_statement` on project status, read vs. write permission (`perm.read?`, project.rb:192-196)
- requires the owning **module enabled** via `EXISTS … enabled_modules` (project.rb:197-203) unless `:skip_pre_condition`
- `admin?` ⇒ returns base unconditionally (project.rb:209-210)
- otherwise builds `statement_by_role`: the **builtin (non-member/anonymous) role** branch checking `is_public` and "not already a member" (project.rb:213-227), plus **`user.project_ids_by_role`** giving `projects.id IN (...)` per member role (project.rb:228-232)
- empty ⇒ `1=0` (project.rb:233-234)
- **the yielded block runs here** (project.rb:236-242): each role's project-statement is `AND`-ed with the issue-SQL the block returned, then all roles OR-ed together (project.rb:243).

`Project.visible_condition` (project.rb:178-180) is just `allowed_to_condition(user, :view_project)` — the same machine, no block.

## 3. The per-record check — `Issue#visible?`
`app/models/issue.rb:172-194`. Calls **`User#allowed_to?(:view_issues, self.project)` with a block** (issue.rb:173). 

**`User#allowed_to?`** — `app/models/user.rb:776-812`:
- Project context: `return false unless context.allows_to?(action)` (module-enabled check, user.rb:778); `admin?` ⇒ true (user.rb:780); else `roles_for_project(context)` (user.rb:782) and **any role** where `(public || role.member?) && role.allowed_to?(action) && yield(role,self)` (user.rb:785-789).
- The **block** (issue.rb:174-192) is the boolean mirror of the SQL block: same `issues_visibility` cases, using `self.author == user` and **`user.is_or_belongs_to?(assigned_to)`** (user.rb:743) for group-aware "own/assigned", and `role.permissions_tracker_ids?(:view_issues, tracker_id)` (issue.rb:189-190) for the tracker narrowing.

So the SQL `assigned_to_id IN (U+group_ids)` (issue.rb:148/150) and the Ruby `is_or_belongs_to?` (issue.rb:180/182) are **the same idea in two encodings** — the canonical divergence risk.

## 4. Where the same visibility idea recurs on other records
The `<Model>.visible_condition` + `<Model>#visible?` pair is a **codebase-wide pattern**, all built on `Project.allowed_to_condition` / `User#allowed_to?` with their own permission + private-flag twist:

**Records whose visibility is defined *relative to issues* (will move when Issue moves):**
- `app/models/journal.rb:73` (`where(Issue.visible_condition…)`) and `journal.rb:165` `visible?` — journals inherit issue visibility, plus a `private_notes` + `view_private_notes` overlay.
- `app/models/time_entry.rb:87` `visible_condition` (embeds `Issue.visible_condition` at `time_entry.rb:70`) and `time_entry.rb:100` `visible?` — permission `:view_time_entries`, own/all split mirroring issues (`time_entries_visibility`).
- `app/models/issue_relation.rb:96,103`; `app/models/issue_custom_field.rb:42`.

**Same pattern, independent permission (parallel implementations to stay consistent with):**
- `project.rb:168,178`; `version.rb:198`; `document.rb:55`; `board.rb:41`; `message.rb:78`; `news.rb:53`; `attachment.rb:205`; `wiki.rb:36` / `wiki_page.rb:89` / `wiki_content.rb:46`; `query.rb:413`; `principal.rb:150`; `custom_value.rb:42` / `custom_field_value.rb:42`; the query subclasses `user_query.rb:77`, `project_admin_query.rb:36`.

## The crux for the planned change
1. **Single source of rule, two emitters.** The `issues_visibility`/tracker/private logic is written twice — SQL block (issue.rb:139-167) and Ruby block (issue.rb:174-192). Any rule change must touch both, identically; they're ~50 lines apart and easy to half-edit.
2. **`Project.allowed_to_condition` (project.rb:189-246) is the universal chokepoint** — both the bulk path and (via `User#allowed_to?` → `roles_for_project`/`allows_to?`) the per-record path resolve project+role+module here. A change centralized at this seam propagates to every `visible_condition` consumer at once; a change made only in `Issue` does not.
3. **Group expansion is load-bearing**: SQL uses `[user.id] + user.groups.pluck(:id)` (issue.rb:145-150); Ruby uses `is_or_belongs_to?` (user.rb:743). Preserve both.
4. **Admin and module-enabled short-circuits** live in the authority layer (project.rb:209, user.rb:780, plus the `EXISTS enabled_modules` clause project.rb:197-203 / `context.allows_to?` user.rb:778) — not in `Issue`. Any reimplementation that bypasses `allowed_to_condition` must re-add these or it will leak issues from disabled modules / closed projects.

Next (Task 5) I'll map how this is enforced at the controller boundary — `application_controller.rb`'s `authorize` / `find_issue(s)` filters — to see the request-time entry points that wrap these model checks.

def available_columns
    return @available_columns if @available_columns

    @available_columns = self.class.available_columns.dup
    @available_columns += issue_custom_fields.visible.collect {|cf| QueryCustomFieldColumn.new(cf)}

    if User.current.allowed_to?(:view_time_entries, project, :global => true)
      # insert the columns after total_estimated_hours or at the end
      index = @available_columns.find_index {|column| column.name == :total_estimated_hours}
      index = (index ? index + 1 : -1)

      subselect = "SELECT SUM(hours) FROM #{TimeEntry.table_name}" +
        " JOIN #{Project.table_name} ON #{Project.table_name}.id = #{TimeEntry.table_name}.project_id" +
        " WHERE (#{TimeEntry.visible_condition(User.current)}) AND #{TimeEntry.table_name}.issue_id = #{Issue.table_name}.id"

      @available_columns.insert(
        index,
        QueryColumn.new(:spent_hours,
                        :sortable => "COALESCE((#{subselect}), 0)",
                        :default_order => 'desc',
                        :caption => :label_spent_time,
                        :totalable => true)
      )

      subselect = "SELECT SUM(hours) FROM #{TimeEntry.table_name}" +
        " JOIN #{Project.table_name} ON #{Project.table_name}.id = #{TimeEntry.table_name}.project_id" +
        " JOIN #{Issue.table_name} subtasks ON subtasks.id = #{TimeEntry.table_name}.issue_id" +
        " WHERE (#{TimeEntry.visible_condition(User.current)})" +
        " AND subtasks.root_id = #{Issue.table_name}.root_id AND subtasks.lft >= #{Issue.table_name}.lft AND subtasks.rgt <= #{Issue.table_name}.rgt"

      @available_columns.insert(
        index + 1,
        QueryColumn.new(:total_spent_hours,
                        :sortable => "COALESCE((#{subselect}), 0)",
                        :default_order => 'desc',
                        :caption => :label_total_spent_time)
      )
    end

    if User.current.allowed_to?(:set_issues_private, nil, :global => true) ||
      User.current.allowed_to?(:set_own_issues_private, nil, :global => true)
      @available_columns <<
        QueryColumn.new(:is_private,
                        :sortable => "#{Issue.table_name}.is_private", :groupable => true)
    end

    disabled_fields = Tracker.disabled_core_fields(trackers).map {|field| field.delete_suffix('_id')}
    if disabled_fields.include?("estimated_hours")
      disabled_fields += %w[total_estimated_hours estimated_remaining_hours]
    end
    @available_columns.reject! do |column|
      disabled_fields.include?(column.name.to_s)
    end

    @available_columns
  end

def trackers
    @trackers ||= (project.nil? ? Tracker.all : project.rolled_up_trackers).visible.sorted
  end

  # Returns a hash of localized labels for all filter operators
  def self.operators_labels
    operators.transform_values {|label| l(*label)}
  end

  # Returns a representation of the available filters for JSON serialization
  def available_filters_as_json
    json = {}
    available_filters.each do |field, filter|
      options = {:type => filter[:type], :name => filter[:name]}
      options[:remote] = true if filter.remote

      if has_filter?(field) || !filter.remote
        options[:values] = filter.values
        if options[:values] && values_for(field)
          missing = Array(values_for(field)).select(&:present?) - options[:values].pluck(1)

def spent_hours
    @spent_hours ||= time_entries.sum(:hours) || 0.0
  end

  # Returns the total number of hours spent on this issue and its descendants
  def total_spent_hours
    @total_spent_hours ||=
      if leaf?
        spent_hours
      else
        self_and_descendants.joins(:time_entries).sum("#{TimeEntry.table_name}.hours").to_f
      end
  end

  def total_estimated_hours
    if leaf?
      estimated_hours
    else
      @total_estimated_hours ||= self_and_descendants.visible.sum(:estimated_hours)
    end
  end

  # Returns the number of estimated remaining hours on this issue
  def estimated_remaining_hours
    (estimated_hours || 0) * (100 - (done_ratio || 0)) / 100
  end

  def relations
    @relations ||= IssueRelation::Relations.new(
      self,
      IssueRelation.where('issue_from_id = ? OR issue_to_id = ?', id, id).sort
    )
  end

  def last_updated_by
    if @last_updated_by
      @last_updated_by.presence
    else
      journals.reorder(:id => :desc).first.try(:user)
    end
  end

  def last_notes
    @last_notes || journals.visible.where.not(notes: '').reorder(:id => :desc).first.try(:notes)
  end

  # Preloads relations for a collection of issues
  def self.load_relations(issues)
    if issues.any?
      relations =
        IssueRelation.where(
          "issue_from_id IN (:ids) OR issue_to_id IN (:ids)", :ids => issues.map(&:id)
        ).all
      issues.each do |issue|
        issue.instance_variable_set(
          :@relations,
          relations.select {|r| r.issue_from_id == issue.id || r.issue_to_id == issue.id}
        )
      end
    end
  end

  # Preloads visible spent time for a collection of issues
  def self.load_visible_spent_hours(issues, user=User.current)
    if issues.any?
      hours_by_issue_id = TimeEntry.visible(user).where(:issue_id => issues.map(&:id)).group(:issue_id).sum(:hours)
      issues.each do |issue|
        issue.instance_variable_set :@spent_hours, (hours_by_issue_id[issue.id] || 0.0)
      end
    end
  end

  # Preloads visible total spent time for a collection of issues
  def self.load_visible_total_spent_hours(issues, user=User.current)
    if issues.any?
      hours_by_issue_id = TimeEntry.visible(user).joins(:issue).
        joins("JOIN #{Issue.table_name} parent ON parent.root_id = #{Issue.table_name}.root_id" +
          " AND parent.lft <= #{Issue.table_name}.lft AND parent.rgt >= #{Issue.table_name}.rgt").
        where("parent.id IN (?)", issues.map(&:id)).group("parent.id").sum(:hours)
      issues.each do |issue|
        issue.instance_variable_set :@total_spent_hours, (hours_by_issue_id[issue.id] || 0.0)
      end
    end
  end

scope :visible, (lambda do |*args|
    joins(:project).
    where(TimeEntry.visible_condition(args.shift || User.current, *args))
  end)
  scope :left_join_issue, (lambda do
    joins(
      "LEFT OUTER JOIN #{Issue.table_name}" \
      " ON #{Issue.table_name}.id = #{TimeEntry.table_name}.issue_id" \
      " AND (#{Issue.visible_condition(User.current)})"
    )
  end)
  scope :on_issue, (lambda do |issue|
    joins(:issue).
    where("#{Issue.table_name}.root_id = #{issue.root_id} AND #{Issue.table_name}.lft >= #{issue.lft} AND #{Issue.table_name}.rgt <= #{issue.rgt}")
  end)

  safe_attributes 'user_id', 'hours', 'comments', 'project_id',
                  'issue_id', 'activity_id', 'spent_on',
                  'custom_field_values', 'custom_fields'

  def webhook_payload_api_template
    "app/views/timelog/show.api.rsb"
  end

  # Returns a SQL conditions string used to find all time entries visible by the specified user
  def self.visible_condition(user, options={})
    Project.allowed_to_condition(user, :view_time_entries, options) do |role, user|
      if role.time_entries_visibility == 'all'
        nil
      elsif role.time_entries_visibility == 'own' && user.id && user.logged?
        "#{table_name}.user_id = #{user.id}"
      else
        '1=0'
      end
    end
  end

  # Returns true if user or current user is allowed to view the time entry
  def visible?(user=nil)
    (user || User.current).allowed_to?(:view_time_entries, self.project) do |role, user|
      if role.time_entries_visibility == 'all'
        true
      elsif role.time_entries_visibility == 'own'
        self.user == user
      else
        false
      end
    end
  end

  def initialize(attributes=nil, *args)
    super

content.text:
Here's the time-tracking model and aggregation trace.

## How a time record connects — `app/models/time_entry.rb`
`TimeEntry` carries **both** an issue and a project link directly (denormalized):
- `belongs_to :project` (time_entry.rb:24) — **always present**, `project_id` is `validates_presence_of` (time_entry.rb:51)
- `belongs_to :issue` (time_entry.rb:25) — **optional** unless `Setting.timelog_required_fields` includes `'issue_id'` (validation time_entry.rb:52)
- plus `belongs_to :user` / `:author` (26-27) and `:activity` (28)

So time can be logged against a project alone or against an issue. When against an issue, `project_id` is set independently (the issue's project, kept in sync) — that's why aggregation can JOIN either way. The inverse side is `Issue has_many :time_entries, :dependent => :destroy` (issue.rb:42 from Task 2).

Helper scopes for issue-scoped rollup: `on_issue` uses the **nested-set bounds** (`root_id`/`lft`/`rgt`) to pull an issue *and its descendants'* entries (time_entry.rb:73-76); `left_join_issue` joins issues gated by `Issue.visible_condition` (time_entry.rb:66-72).

## Where spent-hours totals are computed — two families, **different visibility behavior**

### A. Per-issue instance methods — `app/models/issue.rb` — **NO visibility gate**
- `Issue#spent_hours` (issue.rb:1178-1180): `time_entries.sum(:hours)` — raw association sum, every entry on the issue regardless of viewer.
- `Issue#total_spent_hours` (issue.rb:1183-1190): leaf ⇒ `spent_hours`; otherwise `self_and_descendants.joins(:time_entries).sum(hours)` (nested-set rollup) — again **unfiltered**.
- Memoized in `@spent_hours`/`@total_spent_hours`, reset in `reload`-path (issue.rb:280-281).

These are the totals shown on the **single issue detail page**, where the viewer already passed `view_issues` + `view_time_entries` on that project, so no per-row filtering is applied.

### B. Bulk/list preloaders — `app/models/issue.rb` — **visibility-gated**
- `Issue.load_visible_spent_hours(issues, user)` (issue.rb:1241-1248): aggregates via **`TimeEntry.visible(user)`** then back-fills `@spent_hours` per issue.
- `Issue.load_visible_total_spent_hours(issues, user)` (issue.rb:1251-1261): same, `TimeEntry.visible(user).joins(:issue)` + nested-set parent JOIN, back-fills `@total_spent_hours`.

**Critical subtlety:** these overwrite the same `@spent_hours`/`@total_spent_hours` ivars that the unfiltered instance methods (A) lazily populate. So the *value depends on whether a preloader ran first* — the issue list calls the `load_visible_*` preloaders, making list totals viewer-filtered; the detail page uses the bare methods, unfiltered. Same accessor, two semantics.

### C. Query-column subselects — `app/models/issue_query.rb:308-339` (from Task 5) — **visibility-gated**
The `:spent_hours`/`:total_spent_hours` sortable columns embed `TimeEntry.visible_condition(User.current)` as raw SQL (issue_query.rb:315, 329), and the whole block is gated by `allowed_to?(:view_time_entries, project, :global => true)` (issue_query.rb:308).

## The visibility/permission gate itself — `TimeEntry.visible` / `visible_condition`
`scope :visible` (time_entry.rb:62-65) wraps `TimeEntry.visible_condition` (time_entry.rb:87-97), which — exactly like `Issue.visible_condition` from Task 4 — delegates to **`Project.allowed_to_condition(user, :view_time_entries)`** with a block branching on **`role.time_entries_visibility`**:
- `'all'` ⇒ no extra restriction (block returns `nil`, time_entry.rb:90)
- `'own'` ⇒ `time_entries.user_id = <user.id>` (time_entry.rb:92)
- else ⇒ `1=0`

The per-record mirror is `TimeEntry#visible?` (time_entry.rb:100-110), the boolean twin (same divergence-risk pattern as Issue: SQL form + Ruby form must agree).

## Notes for the planned change
1. **`view_time_entries` + `time_entries_visibility` are a second, parallel axis** to `view_issues`/`issues_visibility` — same `Project.allowed_to_condition` chokepoint (Task 4), same `'all'/'own'` role-column shape. A unified visibility rework should treat both together.
2. **Aggregation visibility is opt-in, not enforced.** `Issue#spent_hours`/`total_spent_hours` (A) bypass `TimeEntry.visible` entirely; only the `load_visible_*` preloaders (B) and query columns (C) apply it. If your change tightens time-entry visibility, the **detail-page totals (A) will still leak hidden entries' hours** unless you route them through the visible scope too. This asymmetry is the easiest regression to ship.
3. **Nested-set coupling:** total rollups depend on `root_id`/`lft`/`rgt` (issue.rb:1188, 1254-1255; time_entry.rb:75) — any visibility SQL that changes issue table aliasing must keep these joins intact.

Next (Task 7) I'll trace the notification/webhook side — how `Mailer` and `acts_as_webhookable` decide *who* receives issue changes — since that's a third place visibility rules are (or should be) applied.

  def relation_removed(relation)
    if current_journal
      current_journal.journalize_relation(relation, :removed)
      current_journal.save
    end
  end

  # Default assignment based on project or category
  def default_assign
    if assigned_to.nil?
      if category && category.assigned_to
        self.assigned_to = category.assigned_to
      elsif project && project.default_assigned_to
        self.assigned_to = project.default_assigned_to
      end
    end
  end

def issue_add(user, issue)
    redmine_headers 'Project' => issue.project.identifier,
                    'Issue-Tracker' => issue.tracker.name,
                    'Issue-Id' => issue.id,
                    'Issue-Author' => issue.author.login,
                    'Issue-Assignee' => assignee_for_header(issue)
    redmine_headers 'Issue-Priority' => issue.priority.name if issue.priority
    message_id issue
    references issue
    @author = issue.author
    @issue = issue
    @user = user
    @issue_url = url_for(:controller => 'issues', :action => 'show', :id => issue)
    subject = "[#{issue.project.name} - #{issue.tracker.name} ##{issue.id}]"
    subject += " (#{issue.status.name})" if Setting.show_status_changes_in_mail_subject?
    subject += " #{issue.subject}"
    mail :to => user,
      :subject => subject
  end

  # Notifies users about a new issue.
  #
  # Example:
  #   Mailer.deliver_issue_add(issue)
  def self.deliver_issue_add(issue)
    users = issue.notified_users | issue.notified_watchers | issue.notified_mentions
    users.each do |user|
      issue_add(user, issue).deliver_later
    end
  end

  # Builds a mail for notifying user about an issue update
  def issue_edit(user, journal)
    issue = journal.journalized
    redmine_headers 'Project' => issue.project.identifier,
                    'Issue-Tracker' => issue.tracker.name,
                    'Issue-Id' => issue.id,
                    'Issue-Author' => issue.author.login,
                    'Issue-Assignee' => assignee_for_header(issue)
    redmine_headers 'Issue-Priority' => issue.priority.name if issue.priority
    message_id journal
    references issue
    @author = journal.user
    s = "[#{issue.project.name} - #{issue.tracker.name} ##{issue.id}] "
    s += "(#{issue.status.name}) " if journal.new_value_for('status_id') && Setting.show_status_changes_in_mail_subject?
    s += issue.subject
    @issue = issue
    @user = user
    @journal = journal
    @journal_details = journal.visible_details
    @issue_url = url_for(:controller => 'issues', :action => 'show', :id => issue, :anchor => "change-#{journal.id}")

    mail :to => user,
      :subject => s
  end

  # Notifies users about an issue update.
  #
  # Example:
  #   Mailer.deliver_issue_edit(journal)
  def self.deliver_issue_edit(journal)
    users  = journal.notified_users | journal.notified_watchers | journal.notified_mentions | journal.journalized.notified_mentions
    users.select! do |user|
      journal.notes? || journal.visible_details(user).any?
    end
    users.each do |user|
      issue_edit(user, journal).deliver_later
    end
  end

  # Builds a mail to user about a new document.
  def document_added(user, document, author)
    redmine_headers 'Project' => document.project.identifier
    @author = author
    @document = document
    @user = user
    @document_url = url_for(:controller => 'documents', :action => 'show', :id => document)
    mail :to => user,
      :subject => "[#{document.project.name}] #{l(:label_document_new)}: #{document.title}"
  end

  # Notifies users that document was created by author
  #
  # Example:
  #   Mailer.deliver_document_added(document, author)
  def self.deliver_document_added(document, author)
    users = document.notified_users
    users.each do |user|
      document_added(user, document, author).deliver_later
    end

def notify?
    @notify != false
  end

  def notify=(arg)
    @notify = arg
  end

  def notified_users
    notified = journalized.notified_users
    if private_notes?
      notified = notified.select {|user| user.allowed_to?(:view_private_notes, journalized.project)}
    end
    notified
  end

  def recipients
    notified_users.map(&:mail)
  end

  def notified_watchers
    notified = journalized.notified_watchers
    select_journal_visible_user(notified)
  end

  def notified_mentions
    notified = super
    select_journal_visible_user(notified)
  end

def send_notification
    if notify? &&
        (
          Setting.notified_events.include?('issue_updated') ||
          (Setting.notified_events.include?('issue_note_added') && notes.present?) ||
          (Setting.notified_events.include?('issue_status_updated') && new_status.present?) ||
          (Setting.notified_events.include?('issue_assigned_to_updated') && detail_for_attribute('assigned_to_id').present?) ||
          (Setting.notified_events.include?('issue_priority_updated') && new_value_for('priority_id').present?) ||
          (Setting.notified_events.include?('issue_fixed_version_updated') && detail_for_attribute('fixed_version_id').present?) ||
          (Setting.notified_events.include?('issue_attachment_added') && details.any? {|d| d.property == 'attachment' && d.value })
        )
      Mailer.deliver_issue_edit(self)
    end
  end

def notify_about?(object)
    if mail_notification == 'all'
      true
    elsif mail_notification.blank? || mail_notification == 'none'
      false
    else
      case object
      when Issue
        case mail_notification
        when 'selected', 'only_my_events'
          # user receives notifications for created/assigned issues on unselected projects
          object.author == self || is_or_belongs_to?(object.assigned_to) || is_or_belongs_to?(object.previous_assignee)
        when 'only_assigned'
          is_or_belongs_to?(object.assigned_to) || is_or_belongs_to?(object.previous_assignee)
        when 'only_owner'
          object.author == self
        when 'only_my_watches'
          object.watched_by?(self)
        end
      when News
        # always send to project members except when mail_notification is set to 'none'
        true
      end
    end
  end

  def notify_about_high_priority_issues?
    self.pref.notify_about_high_priority_issues

content.text:
Here's the full notification trace, split by **create** and **update**, in execution order.

## TRIGGER — what fires it

**On create** (issue.rb):
1. `after_create_commit :send_notification` (issue.rb:132) — registered *before* `add_auto_watcher` (133) so that, because `after_*` callbacks run in **inverse registration order**, the auto-watcher is added *before* the mail is sent (explicit comment, issue.rb:129-131).
2. `Issue#send_notification` (issue.rb:2099-2103): gated by `notify?` (issue.rb:1169, the `@notify` flag) **AND** `Setting.notified_events.include?('issue_added')`. Then calls `Mailer.deliver_issue_add(self)`.

**On update** — the trigger hangs off the **Journal**, not the Issue:
1. Issue edit creates a journal via `init_journal`/`current_journal` (issue.rb:881-887) and saves it (`create_journal` is in the `after_save` chain, issue.rb:126).
2. `Journal after_create_commit :send_notification` (journal.rb:66).
3. `Journal#send_notification` (journal.rb:374-387): gated by `notify?` **AND** a per-event check against `Setting.notified_events` — `issue_updated`, or `issue_note_added` (notes present), `issue_status_updated`, `issue_assigned_to_updated`, `issue_priority_updated`, `issue_fixed_version_updated`, `issue_attachment_added` (journal.rb:377-383). Then calls `Mailer.deliver_issue_edit(self)`.

So: **create → Issue commit; update → Journal commit.** Both are `after_*_commit`, i.e. fire only after the DB transaction commits.

## COMPOSE — where the mail is built — `app/models/mailer.rb`

**Create:** `Mailer.deliver_issue_add(issue)` (mailer.rb:105-110) → for each recipient builds `issue_add(user, issue)` (mailer.rb:81-99) → `.deliver_later` (mailer.rb:108) — enqueued as an ActiveJob (`ActionMailer::MailDeliveryJob`), not sent inline.

**Update:** `Mailer.deliver_issue_edit(journal)` (mailer.rb:141-149) → `issue_edit(user, journal)` (mailer.rb:113-135) → `.deliver_later` (mailer.rb:147). Note the per-user `@journal_details = journal.visible_details` (mailer.rb:130) — the rendered body itself is filtered per recipient.

## DECIDE WHO — recipient resolution (the visibility-critical part)

**Create** — recipient set is a union (mailer.rb:106):
```
issue.notified_users | issue.notified_watchers | issue.notified_mentions
```

`Issue#notified_users` (issue.rb:1147-1161), in order:
1. Seed with `author, assigned_to, previous_assignee` (issue.rb:1151).
2. Expand groups to their users (issue.rb:1152) — group assignees fan out.
3. Keep only `u.active? && u.notify_about?(self)` (issue.rb:1154).
4. Add `project.notified_users` (issue.rb:1156) and, if `priority.high?`, project users with `notify_about_high_priority_issues?` (issue.rb:1157).
5. **`notified.reject! {|user| !visible?(user)}`** (issue.rb:1160) — **the visibility gate**: every candidate must pass `Issue#visible?` (Task 4). This is where private/`issues_visibility`/tracker rules cut the recipient list.

`User#notify_about?(issue)` (user.rb:864-888) applies the user's own preference: `all`/`none`, or `selected`/`only_my_events`/`only_assigned`/`only_owner`/`only_my_watches` — using `is_or_belongs_to?` for author/assignee/previous-assignee and `watched_by?` (user.rb:870-882).

**Update** — union is broader (mailer.rb:142):
```
journal.notified_users | journal.notified_watchers | journal.notified_mentions | journal.journalized.notified_mentions
```
then `select! { journal.notes? || journal.visible_details(user).any? }` (mailer.rb:143-145) — a recipient is dropped if, after per-user detail filtering, there's nothing they're allowed to see.

`Journal#notified_users` (journal.rb:186-192): starts from `journalized.notified_users` (i.e. the Issue method above, **so the `visible?` gate already applied**), then **if `private_notes?`, further restricts to users with `:view_private_notes` permission** (journal.rb:189) — a *second* permission gate layered on top of issue visibility. `notified_watchers`/`notified_mentions` (journal.rb:198-206) additionally pass through `select_journal_visible_user`.

## Execution order summary

**Create:** save issue → commit → (auto-watcher added) → `Issue#send_notification` → event/notify gate → `deliver_issue_add` → build `notified_users ∪ watchers ∪ mentions` → filter by `active? + notify_about? + visible?` → `issue_add` per user → `deliver_later`.

**Update:** save issue + journal → journal commit → `Journal#send_notification` → per-event gate → `deliver_issue_edit` → union of journal users/watchers/mentions → filter by `visible?` (inherited) + `view_private_notes` (if private notes) + has-visible-content → `issue_edit` per user → `deliver_later`.

## Notes for the planned change
1. **`Issue#visible?` is the single recipient gate** (issue.rb:1160). Any change to visibility semantics silently changes *who gets email*. This is a non-obvious dependent of the visibility rule — not in the Task 3 grep for `Issue.visible`/`visible_condition` because it calls the instance `visible?`.
2. **Private notes add a second axis**: `:view_private_notes` (journal.rb:189) — orthogonal to issue visibility, must be considered alongside it.
3. **Per-user body filtering** via `visible_details(user)` (mailer.rb:130, 144) means visibility is enforced at *two* granularities: recipient inclusion *and* which change-details render — both consume the permission layer.
4. Delivery is async (`deliver_later`) — recipient computation happens **synchronously at commit** (current `User.current`/state), but the mail renders later in a job; `@journal_details` is captured at compose time, so the snapshot is taken when the job builds the mail.

Next (Task 8) I'll look at the controller request boundary (`application_controller.rb` `find_issue`/`authorize`) so the map covers how all these model gates are invoked per HTTP request.

module Redmine
  module Scm
    class Base
      class << self
        def all
          @scms || []
        end

        # Add a new SCM adapter and repository
        def add(scm_name)
          @scms ||= []
          @scms.delete(scm_name)
          @scms << scm_name
        end

        # Remove a SCM adapter from Redmine's list of supported scms
        def delete(scm_name)
          @scms.delete(scm_name)
        end
      end
    end
  end
end

content.text:
Here's the version-control dispatch architecture. It's a **two-layer design**: STI persistence models (`Repository::*`) that each point at a stateless adapter (`Redmine::Scm::Adapters::*Adapter`), wired through a small runtime registry.

## Layer 1 — Repository STI models (persistence + Redmine-domain logic)
- **Shared base:** `app/models/repository.rb` — `class Repository < ApplicationRecord`, single-table inheritance keyed on the `type` column.
- **Per-system subclasses:** `app/models/repository/{git,subversion,mercurial,cvs,bazaar,filesystem}.rb` — e.g. `class Repository::Git < Repository` (git.rb:23).

## Layer 2 — SCM adapters (stateless shell-command wrappers)
- **Shared base:** `lib/redmine/scm/adapters/abstract_adapter.rb` (`Redmine::Scm::Adapters::AbstractAdapter`).
- **Per-system adapters:** `lib/redmine/scm/adapters/{git,subversion,mercurial,cvs,bazaar,filesystem}_adapter.rb` (Mercurial also has a `lib/redmine/scm/mercurial/` helper dir).

## The dispatch chain — how a model reaches the right backend
1. **Model → adapter class.** Each subclass overrides `self.scm_adapter_class`. For Git, `repository/git.rb:34-36` returns `Redmine::Scm::Adapters::GitAdapter`. The base default is `nil` (repository.rb:407-409). The instance forwards via `Repository#scm_adapter` (repository.rb:98-100) → `self.class.scm_adapter_class`.
2. **Adapter instantiation.** `Repository#scm` (repository.rb:102-111) lazily news up `self.scm_adapter.new(url, root_url, login, password, path_encoding)` and memoizes `@scm`. This is the single seam where the polymorphic model hands off to the system-specific backend.
3. **Operation dispatch.** Domain operations call `scm.<op>` and let STI + the memoized adapter resolve the system. E.g. fetching changesets: the controller-driven `Repository#fetch_changesets` is **abstract in the base** (overridden per subclass — `repository/git.rb:132`), and each subclass implementation walks `scm.revisions(...)` / `scm.branches` etc. against its adapter. The batch entrypoint `Repository.fetch_changesets` (class method, repository.rb:371) iterates all repositories and calls each instance's polymorphic `fetch_changesets`.
4. **Capability flags** are likewise virtual on the base: `supports_cat?`, `supports_annotate?`, `supports_history?`, `supports_directory_revisions?`, `supports_revision_graph?` (repository.rb:177-193) — subclasses/adapters answer per system.

## The registry — name ⇄ class resolution
- `lib/redmine/scm/base.rb` (`Redmine::Scm::Base`) is a tiny class-level registry: `add(scm_name)` appends (repository.rb's `@scms`, base.rb:29-33), `all` lists (base.rb:24-26), `delete` removes (base.rb:36-38). Each SCM registers its short name here at load (and plugins use the same `add`/`delete` to extend or restrict the supported set).
- `Repository.repository_class(class_name)` (repository.rb:400-405) camelizes a name and, **only if it's in `Redmine::Scm::Base.all`**, resolves `"Repository::#{class_name}".constantize` — this is the guard that maps a user-supplied/stored SCM name to the concrete STI subclass.
- `Repository.factory(klass_name, *)` (repository.rb:396) and `available_scm` (`subclasses.collect { [klass.scm_name, klass.name] }`, repository.rb:393) drive the new-repository form and instantiation.
- `self.scm_name` per subclass gives the registry key (e.g. `'Git'`, git.rb:38-40); base default is the class basename (repository.rb:388).

## Adapter availability passthrough
The base proxies environment/tooling questions to the adapter class: `scm_command` (repository.rb:411), `scm_version_string` (421), `scm_available` (431) each call `self.scm_adapter_class.client_command/client_version_string/client_available` — so "is git installed?" is answered by `GitAdapter`, not the model.

## Summary of where things live
| Concern | Shared base | Per-system |
|---|---|---|
| Persistence / domain (STI) | `app/models/repository.rb` | `app/models/repository/<scm>.rb` |
| Backend command wrapper | `lib/redmine/scm/adapters/abstract_adapter.rb` | `lib/redmine/scm/adapters/<scm>_adapter.rb` |

Here's the version-control dispatch architecture. It's a **two-layer design**: STI persistence models (`Repository::*`) that each point at a stateless adapter (`Redmine::Scm::Adapters::*Adapter`), wired through a small runtime registry.

## Layer 1 — Repository STI models (persistence + Redmine-domain logic)
- **Shared base:** `app/models/repository.rb` — `class Repository < ApplicationRecord`, single-table inheritance keyed on the `type` column.
- **Per-system subclasses:** `app/models/repository/{git,subversion,mercurial,cvs,bazaar,filesystem}.rb` — e.g. `class Repository::Git < Repository` (git.rb:23).

## Layer 2 — SCM adapters (stateless shell-command wrappers)
- **Shared base:** `lib/redmine/scm/adapters/abstract_adapter.rb` (`Redmine::Scm::Adapters::AbstractAdapter`).
- **Per-system adapters:** `lib/redmine/scm/adapters/{git,subversion,mercurial,cvs,bazaar,filesystem}_adapter.rb` (Mercurial also has a `lib/redmine/scm/mercurial/` helper dir).

## The dispatch chain — how a model reaches the right backend
1. **Model → adapter class.** Each subclass overrides `self.scm_adapter_class`. For Git, `repository/git.rb:34-36` returns `Redmine::Scm::Adapters::GitAdapter`. The base default is `nil` (repository.rb:407-409). The instance forwards via `Repository#scm_adapter` (repository.rb:98-100) → `self.class.scm_adapter_class`.
2. **Adapter instantiation.** `Repository#scm` (repository.rb:102-111) lazily news up `self.scm_adapter.new(url, root_url, login, password, path_encoding)` and memoizes `@scm`. This is the single seam where the polymorphic model hands off to the system-specific backend.
3. **Operation dispatch.** Domain operations call `scm.<op>` and let STI + the memoized adapter resolve the system. E.g. fetching changesets: the controller-driven `Repository#fetch_changesets` is **abstract in the base** (overridden per subclass — `repository/git.rb:132`), and each subclass implementation walks `scm.revisions(...)` / `scm.branches` etc. against its adapter. The batch entrypoint `Repository.fetch_changesets` (class method, repository.rb:371) iterates all repositories and calls each instance's polymorphic `fetch_changesets`.
4. **Capability flags** are likewise virtual on the base: `supports_cat?`, `supports_annotate?`, `supports_history?`, `supports_directory_revisions?`, `supports_revision_graph?` (repository.rb:177-193) — subclasses/adapters answer per system.

## The registry — name ⇄ class resolution
- `lib/redmine/scm/base.rb` (`Redmine::Scm::Base`) is a tiny class-level registry: `add(scm_name)` appends (repository.rb's `@scms`, base.rb:29-33), `all` lists (base.rb:24-26), `delete` removes (base.rb:36-38). Each SCM registers its short name here at load (and plugins use the same `add`/`delete` to extend or restrict the supported set).
- `Repository.repository_class(class_name)` (repository.rb:400-405) camelizes a name and, **only if it's in `Redmine::Scm::Base.all`**, resolves `"Repository::#{class_name}".constantize` — this is the guard that maps a user-supplied/stored SCM name to the concrete STI subclass.
- `Repository.factory(klass_name, *)` (repository.rb:396) and `available_scm` (`subclasses.collect { [klass.scm_name, klass.name] }`, repository.rb:393) drive the new-repository form and instantiation.
- `self.scm_name` per subclass gives the registry key (e.g. `'Git'`, git.rb:38-40); base default is the class basename (repository.rb:388).

## Adapter availability passthrough
The base proxies environment/tooling questions to the adapter class: `scm_command` (repository.rb:411), `scm_version_string` (421), `scm_available` (431) each call `self.scm_adapter_class.client_command/client_version_string/client_available` — so "is git installed?" is answered by `GitAdapter`, not the model.

| Concern | Shared base | Per-system |
|---|---|---|
| Persistence / domain (STI) | `app/models/repository.rb` | `app/models/repository/<scm>.rb` |
| Backend command wrapper | `lib/redmine/scm/adapters/abstract_adapter.rb` | `lib/redmine/scm/adapters/<scm>_adapter.rb` |
| Name⇄class registry | `lib/redmine/scm/base.rb` | each subclass calls `Base.add` + defines `scm_name`/`scm_adapter_class` |

# Impact Map — Issue Visibility & Permissions Change

Synthesized from the full session. Ranked by regression risk. Every entry is something a teammate must re-verify before merge. The organizing fact: **visibility is defined as a rule on `Role` (`issues_visibility` + per-tracker perms + `is_private`), emitted in two forms (SQL `visible_condition` / Ruby `visible?`), funneled through one chokepoint (`Project.allowed_to_condition` / `User#allowed_to?`), and then consumed — often as spliced raw SQL — by a dozen subsystems that never appear in a search for `Issue.visible`.**

---

## TIER 0 — The source of truth (change these deliberately; everything else follows)

| What | file:line | Why it's the core |
|---|---|---|
| **The rule's parameters** | `app/models/role.rb:40-44` (`issues_visibility` enum), `role.rb:204-210` (`allowed_to?`), `role.rb:236-240` (`permissions_tracker_ids?`), `role.rb:251` (`permissions_all_trackers?`) | All visibility branches read these. A new visibility mode = new enum value here + both emitters below. |
| **SQL emitter** | `app/models/issue.rb:137-169` (`Issue.visible_condition`) | Builds the per-role SQL block; branches `all`/`default`/`own` + private + tracker. |
| **Ruby emitter (MUST stay identical)** | `app/models/issue.rb:172-194` (`Issue#visible?`) | Boolean mirror of the SQL, ~30 lines away. **#1 internal divergence risk** — half-editing one is the classic bug. |
| **The chokepoint** | `app/models/project.rb:189-246` (`Project.allowed_to_condition`) + `app/models/user.rb:776-812` (`User#allowed_to?`) | Both emitters delegate here for project-status, module-enabled (`EXISTS enabled_modules`, project.rb:197-203 / `context.allows_to?` user.rb:778), admin short-circuit (project.rb:209, user.rb:780), builtin/non-member roles, and group expansion. Centralize the change HERE to propagate everywhere at once; bypass it and you re-leak closed-project/disabled-module issues. |
| **Group semantics** | SQL: `issue.rb:145-150` (`[user.id]+user.groups.pluck(:id)`); Ruby: `user.rb:743` (`is_or_belongs_to?`) | "own/assigned" must keep group-assignee behavior in both forms. |
| **`scope :visible`** | `app/models/issue.rb:84-88` | Thin wrapper; the public query API everyone calls. |

---

## TIER 1 — Raw-SQL embedders (HIGHEST regression risk: invisible to `Issue.visible` searches; break on SQL-shape/aliasing changes)

These splice `Issue.visible_condition` / `TimeEntry.visible_condition` as **string fragments**. If you change table aliasing, column names, or the SQL structure, these silently break and won't show up in a "find usages."

1. **`app/models/issue_query.rb:62`** — `Issue.visible_condition(...).gsub(/\bissues\b/, 'subtasks')` — **string-rewrites the SQL** for subtask counts. The single most fragile consumer; any alias touching the word `issues` corrupts it.
2. **`app/models/journal.rb:73`** — `where(Issue.visible_condition(user, options))`. Journal visibility *is* issue visibility. Plus the **second permission axis** `:view_private_notes` at `journal.rb:189`.
3. **`app/models/time_entry.rb:70`** — `left_join_issue` embeds `Issue.visible_condition`; **`time_entry.rb:87-97`** (`TimeEntry.visible_condition`) is the parallel time axis on `Project.allowed_to_condition(:view_time_entries)` + `role.time_entries_visibility`.
4. **`app/models/issue_custom_field.rb:42`** — ANDs `Issue.visible_condition(user)` into custom-field value SQL.
5. **`app/models/issue_query.rb:315, 329`** — spent-hours **sort columns** embed `TimeEntry.visible_condition` (which embeds issue visibility). Breaking the SQL shape breaks list sorting, not just rows.
6. **`app/models/issue_query.rb:376, 398`; `issue_query.rb:315/329` time joins** — `Issue.visible.joins(...)` statement assembly.

---

## TIER 2 — The two emitters must agree with their per-record twins (semantic-drift risk)

Every sibling record reimplements the SQL+Ruby pair. If issue visibility semantics shift, these need conscious review for consistency:

- **Time tracking:** `time_entry.rb:87-97` (`visible_condition`) ↔ `time_entry.rb:100-110` (`visible?`) — second axis (`time_entries_visibility` `all`/`own`), same chokepoint.
- **Journals:** `journal.rb:73` ↔ `journal.rb:165` (`visible?`) + private-notes overlay `journal.rb:186-192`.
- **Relations:** `issue_relation.rb:96, 103`.
- **Broader family (same pattern, own permission):** `project.rb:168/178`, `version.rb:198`, `document.rb:55`, `board.rb:41`, `message.rb:78`, `news.rb:53`, `attachment.rb:205`, `wiki*.rb` (36/89/46), `query.rb:413`, `principal.rb:150`, `custom_value.rb:42`/`custom_field_value.rb:42`, query subclasses `user_query.rb:77`/`project_admin_query.rb:36`.

---

## TIER 3 — Non-obvious behavioral dependents (correct SQL, wrong outcome if rule changes)

These call the **instance** `visible?` or rely on visibility indirectly — they do **not** appear in a `visible_condition`/`Issue.visible` grep:

1. **Notifications — recipient gate:** `app/models/issue.rb:1160` (`notified.reject! {|u| !visible?(u)}`) inside `notified_users` (issue.rb:1147-1161). **Visibility changes change who gets email.** Trigger order: create → `issue.rb:132` `after_create_commit :send_notification` → `issue.rb:2099` → `Mailer.deliver_issue_add` (`mailer.rb:105`); update → `journal.rb:66/374` → `Mailer.deliver_issue_edit` (`mailer.rb:141`). Per-user body filtering: `mailer.rb:130, 144` (`visible_details`). Private-notes recipient cut: `journal.rb:189`.
2. **Spent-hours asymmetry (silent leak):** `issue.rb:1178-1190` (`spent_hours`/`total_spent_hours`) — **NO visibility filter** (detail page). vs. `issue.rb:1241-1261` (`load_visible_spent_hours`/`load_visible_total_spent_hours`) — **filtered** (list). They overwrite the **same `@spent_hours` ivars**, so the value depends on whether a preloader ran. Tighten time visibility and the **detail page still leaks hidden hours**.
3. **Query column *presence* is permission-gated:** `issue_query.rb:308` (`:view_time_entries` → spent-hours cols), `issue_query.rb:341-342` (`:set_issues_private`/`:set_own_issues_private` → `is_private` col), `issue_query.rb:306` + `custom_field.rb:56-74` (`CustomField.visible` role-JOIN → which CF columns appear). A permission change adds/removes **columns**, not just rows. Column SQL also reaches `Version.fields_for_order_statement`, nested-set cols, etc. (issue_query.rb:25-78).
4. **Nested-set coupling:** `issue.rb:1188, 1254-1255`; `time_entry.rb:75`; `issue_query.rb:330` use `root_id`/`lft`/`rgt`. Any visibility SQL that re-aliases the issues table must preserve these joins.

---

## TIER 4 — Edit/action gates & mass-assignment (authorization, not just visibility)

If the change touches *permissions* broadly (not only view):
- `issue.rb:202-229` — `editable?`/`attributes_editable?`/`notes_addable?`/`deletable?`/`attachments_editable?` (the last ANDs `visible?`, issue.rb:218-220), all via `user_tracker_permission?`.
- `issue.rb:82` `validate_permissions`; `issue.rb:498-537` permission-keyed `safe_attributes` blocks (`Redmine::SafeAttributes`).
- `time_loggable?` issue.rb:197; `Issue#visible?`-dependent attachment editing.

---

## TIER 5 — Surface consumers of `Issue.visible` scope (will behave correctly if Tier 0 is right; verify counts/redirects)

Controllers: `issues_controller.rb:425, 597`; `projects_controller.rb:186-191`; `users_controller.rb:97-106`; `versions_controller.rb:53`; `previews_controller.rb:25`; `repositories_controller.rb:265`; `search_controller.rb:41`; `application_controller.rb:395`.
Models/helpers/lib: `issue_relation.rb:96`; `issue_query.rb:376`; `application_helper.rb:1195`; `issues_helper.rb:584,592`; `my_helper.rb:80`; `wiki_formatting/macros.rb:368`.
Search/activity (independent visibility application): `lib/plugins/acts_as_searchable/lib/acts_as_searchable.rb`; `acts_as_activity_provider` (issue.rb:59); reporting `lib/redmine/helpers/gantt.rb`, `time_report.rb`, `export/pdf/issues_pdf_helper.rb`.

---

## TIER 6 — Version control (mostly orthogonal; one coupling point)

The SCM adapter dispatch (`repository.rb:98-111` → `scm_adapter_class` → `lib/redmine/scm/adapters/*`) is **permission-agnostic** — no change needed there. The only coupling: **changeset↔issue links** are gated by `:view_changesets` via the same `Project.allowed_to_condition` chokepoint, and `repositories_controller.rb:265` uses `Issue.visible`. Verify changeset→issue reference rendering after the change; the adapter layer itself is out of scope.

---

## Suggested scoping order for the teammate
1. **Change Tier 0** (role rule + both emitters + chokepoint) together; add any new mode to `role.rb` enum + both `issue.rb:137`/`issue.rb:172` blocks.
2. **Grep-audit Tier 1** by string (`visible_condition`, not `.visible`) — these won't show in IDE "find usages." Re-verify the `issue_query.rb:62` `.gsub` especially.
3. **Re-test Tier 3 behaviorally:** send a notification (recipient set), load an issue list vs. detail page (spent-hours parity), and a query with time/private columns (column presence).
4. Tier 2 = consistency review; Tier 4 = only if permissions (not just view) change; Tier 5 = smoke-test counts/redirects; Tier 6 = changeset-reference spot check.

**The three traps that ship regressions:** (a) editing the SQL emitter but not the Ruby `visible?` (issue.rb:137 vs 172); (b) the `issue_query.rb:62` `.gsub` and Tier-1 string-splices; (c) the spent-hours visibility asymmetry (issue.rb:1178 unfiltered vs 1241 filtered, shared ivar).

This map plus the Tier-0 chokepoint is enough to scope and land the change.
